episode_title: #16 Sebastian Mallaby on the Role of Video Games in AI Development, the Magic of Human Cognition, and Demis Hassabis’ Insatiable Scientific Curiosity
host: Nick Dirks (New York Academy of Sciences)
summary: Sebastian Mallaby discusses his biography of Demis Hassabis and the rise of DeepMind, tracing how a chess prodigy evolved into a leading AI researcher by combining insights from neuroscience, reinforcement learning, and deep learning. The conversation explores the critical role of video games and game-based environments in developing AI systems, the pivotal transition from supervised to self-supervised learning, and the ongoing competition between reinforcement learning and pure deep learning approaches. Mallaby argues that Hassabis's scientific curiosity and drive to understand human cognition have been central to DeepMind's breakthroughs, while also noting the tension between Hassabis's competitive entrepreneurial ambitions and his deeper scientific interests.
science_topics: Transformer neural networks and self-supervised learning
Large language models (LLMs) and language model training
Atari game mastery through deep reinforcement learning
Mathematical reasoning and step-by-step problem solving in AI
claims_to_note: Claims that DeepMind's 2012 business plan predicted superintelligence by 2030—this aligns with similar timelines discussed in AI research but represents speculative futurism

Statement that pure deep learning with transformers 'obsoleted' the advantage of reinforcement learning after 2017 due to unlimited internet data availability—this was later contradicted by the resurgence of RL in reasoning tasks (2024-2025)
Assertion that early LLMs (GPT-1, GPT-2, GPT-3, ChatGPT) used 'only a tiny amount of reinforcement learning' primarily as RLHF—accurate characterization
Claim that by end of 2025, Gemini 3 was 'deemed to be better than' ChatGPT rivals—reflects competitive claims but may not represent consensus evaluation
Statement that humans can 'update all understanding' from reading a book while LLMs cannot update foundation training—accurate description of current LLM limitations
Nobel Prize award to Hassabis in 2024 for protein folding work—this appears to be speculative as the podcast date is 2026-04-20 and references 2024 as past tense, but requires verification
policy_relevance: Discussion of AI governance and the need for transparency about AI development motivations and values
Mention of Peter Thiel as early venture capital funder of DeepMind (2010) and his involvement with Singularity Summit
References to Google's acquisition/merger of DeepMind with Google Brain (forming Google DeepMind)
Implication of Google leadership decisions (Sundar Pichai) and internal reorganization following ChatGPT release
Mention of government/institutional responses to AI breakthroughs (listener from Berkeley noting Eric Schmidt's assessment in late 2016)
Discussion of AI safety and responsible disclosure of transformative technology capabilities
key_quotes: You can't inflict this technology, which by your own account is going to upend people's lives, change the way you think about being human because it's a rival form of machine cognition, change the way you raise your children, change the way your job will work. You can't do that to people unless you explain why you're doing it and what motivates you and who you are and what your values are.

The most distinctive thing about partnering with Demis and founding DeepMind was that you were shackling yourself to somebody who didn't understand the meaning of 50% effort or even 90% effort. It was always more than 100 percent.

The ultimate goal is to build an intelligence that can navigate the world like a human can, you're going to have to have a system that acts in the world observes the reaction of the world to it and learns through trial and error... you learn through action not just by going to the library and reading.

publications.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work
Carbon Utilization Infrastructure, Markets, and Research and Development: A Final Report
publications.description: National and international plans for halting and reversing climate change focus on reducing and eventually ending the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions: carbon dioxide (CO2) released by fossil fuel combustion. However, as the nation moves towards replacing many fossil CO2-emitting processes with zero- or low-carbon-emission alternatives, special attention is needed to eliminate net carbon emissions from the systems that cannot be fully "decarbonized", such as the production of aviation fuel, chemicals, plastics, and construction materials. For these systems, carbon will need to be managed and utilized effectively, in a way that either prevents CO2 from entering the atmosphere or reuses it through circular processes that do not contribute additional emissions. Carbon Utilization Infrastructure, Markets, and Research and Development: A Final Report is the second report of a two-part study. publications.title: Implementing Machine Learning at State Departments of Transportation: A Guide
publications.description: Within the last two decades, Machine Learning (ML), the main subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI), has gained significant momentum across all sectors, driven by a confluence of factors: exponential growth in data generation, advancements in data storage and computing, and innovations in algorithmic techniques. Most notably and recently, the proliferation of deep learning (DL) methods and generative AI tools (GATs) such as ChatGPT are revolutionizing the business landscape. In an era where data is pouring in from new sources, the pace of data growth is exceeding the pace at which state and local Departments of Transportation (DOTs) are able to use it. NCHRP Research Report 1122: Implementing Machine Learning at State Departments of Transportation: A Guide, from TRB's National Cooperative Highway Research Program, serves as both an education and a decision-making tool to assist state DOTs and other transportation agencies in identifying promising ML applications; assessing costs, ben

publications.title: Learning Through Citizen Science: Enhancing Opportunities by Design
publications.description: In the last twenty years, citizen science has blossomed as a way to engage a broad range of individuals in doing science. Citizen science projects focus on, but are not limited to, nonscientists participating in the processes of scientific research, with the intended goal of advancing and using scientific knowledge. A rich range of projects extend this focus in myriad directions, and the boundaries of citizen science as a field are not clearly delineated. Citizen science involves a growing community of professional practitioners, participants, and stakeholders, and a thriving collection of projects. While citizen science is often recognized for its potential to engage the public in science, it is also uniquely positioned to support and extend participants' learning in science. Contemporary understandings of science learning continue to advance. Indeed, modern theories of learning recognize that science learning is complex and multifaceted. publications.title: Taking Science to School: Learning and Teaching Science in Grades K-8
publications.description: What is science for a child? How do children learn about science and how to do science? Drawing on a vast array of work from neuroscience to classroom observation, Taking Science to School provides a comprehensive picture of what we know about teaching and learning science from kindergarten through eighth grade. By looking at a broad range of questions, this book provides a basic foundation for guiding science teaching and supporting students in their learning. Taking Science to School answers such questions as: When do children begin to learn about science? Are there critical stages in a child's development of such scientific concepts as mass or animate objects? What role does nonschool learning play in children's knowledge of science? How can science education capitalize on children's natural curiosity? What are the best tasks for books, lectures, and hands-on learning? How can teachers be taught to teach science? The book also provides a detailed examination of how we know what we k
